Name: spectral_stability_latency_aware_inference
Title: Spectral Stability Bounds for Training-Free Latency-Aware Inference
Short Hypothesis: Spectral stability bounds can be used to dynamically allocate computational resources during inference, ensuring latency-aware performance without requiring additional training.
Related Work: Existing work has explored mixed-precision inference and geometric interference cancellation, but these approaches often rely on static profiling or costly calibration. Our proposal distinguishes itself by using spectral stability bounds to dynamically allocate resources based on real-time activation geometry, providing a more flexible and efficient solution.
Abstract: We propose a novel framework for training-free, latency-aware inference that leverages spectral stability bounds to dynamically allocate computational resources. By analyzing the spectral properties of hidden states during inference, our method identifies tokens that can be processed with reduced precision or fewer computational steps without sacrificing accuracy. We introduce a spectral stability metric derived from the condition number and spectral decay rate of activation covariances, which serves as a proxy for computational difficulty. This metric guides a dynamic routing mechanism that enforces strict latency budgets while maintaining robust performance. We validate our approach on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, demonstrating superior throughput-accuracy trade-offs compared to existing methods. Our theoretical analysis links spectral stability to generalization stability under computational perturbations, providing a transparent pathway for resource-efficient inference.
Experiments: Benchmark Validation: Evaluate the framework on diverse benchmarks (e.g., WILDS, adversarial ICL) to measure accuracy, latency, and throughput.
Spectral Stability Analysis: Analyze the spectral properties of hidden states and validate the proposed stability metric.
Dynamic Routing Mechanism: Implement and test the dynamic routing mechanism, comparing it to static and heuristic baselines.
Theoretical Analysis: Derive and validate theoretical bounds linking spectral stability to generalization stability under computational perturbations.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Complexity of Spectral Analysis: The spectral analysis required for dynamic routing may introduce computational overhead.
Generalization to Diverse Models: The framework's effectiveness may vary across different model architectures and tasks.
Latency Budget Enforcement: Ensuring strict latency budgets without sacrificing accuracy may be challenging in highly dynamic environments.
